content: Vestibular disorder treatment depends on etiology. BPPV: canalith repositioning maneuvers—Epley maneuver for posterior canal (80-90% effective after 1-3 treatments), Semont maneuver alternative, barbecue roll for horizontal canal. No role for vestibular suppressants long-term. Vestibular neuritis/labyrinthitis: acute phase (first 24-72 hours) vestibular suppressants (meclizine, dimenhydrinate, benzodiazepines) and antiemetics; corticosteroids (prednisone taper) may improve recovery if started within 3 days. After acute phase: discontinue suppressants (impede compensation), start vestibular rehabilitation therapy (gaze stabilization, habituation, balance exercises). Ménière's disease: low-salt diet (<2g/day sodium), diuretics (HCTZ/triamterene), betahistine (H1 agonist/H3 antagonist, widely used outside US, limited evidence). Intratympanic steroids for refractory cases; intratympanic gentamicin for severe refractory vertigo (chemical labyrinthectomy—hearing loss risk). Endolymphatic sac surgery for refractory cases. Vestibular migraine: migraine preventives (topiramate, verapamil, venlafaxine, amitriptyline), trigger management. Bilateral vestibulopathy: vestibular rehabilitation (limited benefit if complete loss), fall prevention, avoid ototoxins. Acoustic neuroma: observation (small, asymptomatic), stereotactic radiosurgery, or microsurgical resection.
